D9 BiologyL5 EvaluateReference

Octopus Skin — Distributed Photoreception

octopus and cuttlefish skin expresses opsin proteins all over the body — the chromatophore array can sample the ambient spectrum independently of the eye, possibly explaining colour matching despite being colour-blind

Ramirez & Oakley 2015 found rhodopsin expression in chromatophore-bearing skin of Octopus bimaculoides. Octopuses have only one cone class (monochromat by retina), yet their camouflage matches ambient colour stunningly. The leading hypothesis: each chromatophore-photoreceptor unit acts as a 1-pixel colour sampler; the array integrates a spatial colour map.

Toggle ambient colour. The octopus skin samples the local spectrum and adjusts chromatophores to match — even without "seeing" through the eyes.
